text: I'm Dr. Karen Zaghian. I'm a colorectal surgeon in Los Angeles and part of the teaching faculty at Cedars-Sinai. Today's case is a laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy for diverticulitis. Our patient's a sixty-five year old woman with recurrent uncomplicated diverticulitis over the past several years, which has progressively worsened over the past year where she's had five discrete attacks in a period of nine months. Therefore, she's elected to proceed with surgery.

Now, while my routine sigmoid colectomy performed laparoscopically involves a laparoscopic ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery, a laparoscopic mobilization of the colon, as well as the splenic flexure, and a laparoscopic distal transection of the rectum, in diverticulitis, this may be tricky due to the significant inflammatory changes we see that are associated with the disease.

Sometimes in diverticulitis surgery, the distal transection has to be performed open so that we can properly assess the distal transection as well as the health of the distal rectum that we are transecting. Another part of this case will be the use of fluorescence angiography to evaluate the proximal colon and its vascular blood supply before we perform the anastomosis. I also perform a routine flexible sigmoidoscopy at the end of the procedure in order to air leak test our anastomosis as well as intraluminally check the anastomosis for defects, blood supply, and bleeding.

The surgeon uses a varus needle to enter the abdomen, followed by the insertion of a ten millimeter trocar in the umbilicus for laparoscopic access. A balloon trocar is used to prevent displacement during the procedure. A ten millimeter, thirty degree scope is employed for enhanced visualization. The abdomen is inspected for any injury from the varus needle, and additional trocars are placed, including a twelve millimeter trocar in the right lower quadrant, positioned two finger breadths from the anterior superior iliac spine (ASIS).

The surgeon positions a five millimeter trocar one hand breadth above the working area, ensuring it does not interfere with other trocars. The patient is placed in the Trendelenburg position with the left side elevated. Using bowel graspers, the surgeon gently sweeps the bowel loops out of the pelvis to minimize trauma.

The surgeon carefully dissects and removes adhesions from the inflamed area of the bowel, ensuring no injury to the adjacent bowel loops. The presence of inflammation and adhesions suggests a possible past abscess.

The surgeon identifies an inflamed segment of the sigmoid colon that needs to be resected. Using a medial to lateral approach, they locate the sacral promontory and the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle, distinguishing it from the iliac branches situated below.

The surgeon holds the tissue up and uses shears to score the area distal to the iliac, identifying the retroperitoneal plane. The inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) pedicle is located, and the ureter is identified to avoid injury to retroperitoneal structures. The surgeon switches to a ten millimeter ligature device for dissection, preferring it for secure vessel ligation.

The surgeon uses a blunt dissecting tool to develop the retroperitoneal plane, revealing the ureter amidst inflamed tissue due to diverticulitis. The ureter is identified, allowing the surgeon to safely sweep down the fat plane above without risking injury.

The surgeon identifies the psoas tendon, indicating a deep dissection into the retroperitoneum. The ureter is swept down along with the retroperitoneum to develop a medial to lateral plane up to the splenic flexure. The peritoneum is carefully opened using a ligature to create more space for dissection, while avoiding the hypogastric nerves.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and prepares to ligate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) using a triple ligation technique. The dissection is performed carefully to avoid injuring the hypogastric nerves, which may not be visible due to inflammation. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is also identified and taken. The surgeon avoids taking the IMA high on its pedicle to minimize the risk of nerve injury, especially in benign disease cases.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and confirms its location before proceeding with the dissection superior to it. The dissection is carried upward to facilitate mobilization and simplify the lateral dissection across the white line of Toldt. The surgeon notes the presence of the psoas muscle, indicating a plane too deep, and adjusts to the correct retroperitoneal plane, which is inflamed and adherent due to previous inflammation. The dissection is bloodier than usual, and the surgeon sweeps from top down to avoid injury, aiming to complete medial dissection to ease lateral work.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure in a female patient with a long segment of diverticulitis affecting a fifteen-centimeter portion of the colon. The medial dissection was challenging, prompting a lateral approach, visible by the air bubble created during the medial dissection. The patient is positioned head down for optimal access to the splenic flexure.

The surgeon identifies the ureter beneath inflamed tissue and applies tension to visualize the dissection area. The dissection progresses medially, encountering adhesions due to diverticulitis, with careful manipulation near the sidewall.

The surgeon carefully navigates the dissection near the ureter, which is visible below the surgical field. They use a cautery device to avoid entering the colon or sidewall, noting the adhesions present. A new trocar is inserted to aid in retraction and suction, facilitating the dissection while avoiding injury to the ureter and colon.

The surgeon carefully performs blunt dissection to separate tissue, ensuring not to cut structures that could be the ureter. The dissection is challenging due to adhesions, and the surgeon considers making a fan and steel incision for better access.

The surgeon mobilizes the splenic flexure by carefully dissecting and pulling the colon over, avoiding splenic injury by hugging the colon and taking down the attachments. The dissection reveals a long segment of inflammation, requiring additional length for the anastomosis.

The surgeon is working to enter the lesser sac to complete the mobilization of the splenic flexure. The stomach is visible above, and care is taken to avoid injury by staying on top of the colon, which is positioned below. The surgeon identifies a bare area and confirms the back wall of the stomach, indicating the lesser sac. Instruments are used to grasp and pull the tissue upwards towards the head, facilitating further dissection.

The surgeon ensures the colon is positioned down in the lesser sac and continues to dissect the area. The colon is pulled over to expose more tissue, and the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is visible and divided near the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The pancreas is located just above in the lesser sac, and the ligament of Treitz is identified medial to the IMV. The surgeon identifies and removes remaining tissue attachments, questioning whether it is mesentery, and proceeds with the dissection.

The surgeon assesses the inflamed sigmoid colon and rectum, noting the presence of adhesions and inflammation extending past the peritoneal reflection. The sigmoid colon appears folded and attached to the perineal reflection or vagina. The surgeon identifies the tinea, indicating the presence of sigmoid colon, and considers making a fan and steel incision due to the inflammation.

The surgeon performs a laparoscopic TAP block using a StimuQuick needle, injecting a weight-based dose of quarter percent bupivacaine with epinephrine at the anterior axillary line, halfway between the costal margin and the ASIS. The needle is inserted through the skin until two pops are felt, indicating entry into the TAP plane, and a bulge forms as the local anesthetic is injected.

The surgeon makes a fan and steel incision, cutting through subcutaneous tissues to expose the anterior sheath and rectus muscle. Subfascial flaps are mobilized to avoid cutting the rectus muscle, using a muscle-sparing technique. The pyramidalis muscle is identified and preserved. The rectus muscle is split, and the peritoneum is grasped. The surgeon uses a bovie to cut through the peritoneum, ensuring the bladder is not injured. An Alexis O wound protector is placed, and the bowel loops are packed away for open work.

The surgeon observes the inflamed colon and notes its folding onto itself, revealing a healthy segment of rectum. The tinea are splayed, indicating the transition from sigmoid colon to rectum. Using a contour stapler with a green load, the surgeon prepares to transect the rectum, ensuring no other tissues are accidentally grasped in the jaws. The colon is aligned to ensure the stapler fires onto the healthy rectum.

The surgeon ensures the vagina is clear of the surgical field before closing the stapler on the colon. The ureter is confirmed to be safe on one side. The contour stapler is fired, and a ligature is used to address remaining mesentery. The inflamed, bulky colon is visible, with the stapler line marking the transition to healthy, soft rectum. Fluorescence angiography with ICG is used to assess the vascularity of the colon, showing a blue hue indicating good blood supply for potential anastomosis.

The surgeon creates a window in the mesentery and uses a ligature to take the remaining meso. The specimen, identified as the sigmoid colon, is passed off. A diverticulum is visible and is excised to prevent weakness in the anastomosis. The surgeon prepares for an open anastomosis, ensuring the mesentery is straight and clear of fat, and positions the EEA stapler for a secure staple line.

The surgeon inserts a 29 EEA stapler through the rectum, ensuring it is not mistakenly placed into the vagina. The stapler is positioned on the staple line, and the mesentery is confirmed to be straight before firing. After firing, the surgeon checks for complete doughnuts, indicating a successful anastomosis. A FlexSig is used to perform a leak test by insufflating air while the anastomosis is submerged in water, confirming no air bubbles and a negative leak test. The anastomosis is noted to be hemostatic, well-perfused, and located low on the rectum, approximately ten centimeters from the anus.
So, three of vikral, and then number one PDS times two, and then monochrome. Can I get a Kool-Aid?
